Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty flexible, maybe too flexible sometimes. I'm a visual learner, so I'd organize my materials with color-coded notes and diagrams, usually spreading things across my desk where I could see everything at once. I tried to dedicate about 13 hours a week to studying, but it wasn't always consistent. Some weeks I'd be really focused, other weeks life got in the way. I found that having all five of my online courses meant I had to juggle a lot, so I'd prioritize based on what was due soonest.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I'd love to say I had a perfect Sunday-to-Saturday schedule, but that wasn't my reality. When assignments were coming up, I'd buckle down and study more intensely. During quieter weeks, I might slack off a bit and focus on my extracurriculars instead. I've learned to work in bursts when I need to, which has helped me climb back from some rough patches academically.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if there were new announcements, looking at upcoming deadlines, or watching lecture videos. I logged in pretty frequently, maybe out of habit at this point. Sometimes I'd just pop in to see if there were any discussion posts I should respond to or if my peers had shared anything useful. The notifications on my phone would also remind me to check in, which helped keep me connected to the course.
Q4: Yeah, around the third week of the semester, logging into the LMS just became something I did every morning with my coffee. It started because I missed an important announcement once and almost messed up an assignment deadline. After that scare, I made it a habit to check in daily, even if just for a few minutes. It became almost automatic, like checking my email or social media. Having reliable internet access made this easy for me to maintain.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely the most useful for me because I'm such a visual learner. I spent a lot of time watching and rewatching them, probably over four hours total. The discussion boards were also helpful because I could see how other students were thinking about the material, which gave me different perspectives. I also appreciated when instructors posted diagrams or infographics because those stuck in my memory way better than walls of text.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skimmed or ignored the longer reading PDFs, especially if they were just text-heavy documents without any visual elements. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant if I'd already understood the concept from the video. I also didn't engage much with some of the optional practice quizzes early on, though I started using them more when exams approached. Time was always a factor in what I chose to prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If it helped me visualize a concept or gave me practice with something I'd be tested on, I was all in. I also valued activities that let me interact with peers because those discussions often clarified things I was confused about. Basically, if I could see the payoff, whether it was understanding or points, I'd engage with it fully.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was less pressure, which was both good and bad. Sometimes that freedom meant I'd explore topics I was genuinely curious about without stress. But other times, knowing something was optional made it easy to push to the bottom of my priority list, especially when required work was piling up. I think I engaged more authentically with optional stuff when I did it, but I did it less consistently.
Q9: Oh absolutely, a lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my laptop so I could study on the bus or at the coffee shop without needing wifi. I also used YouTube tutorials and other external resources when I needed a concept explained differently. My physical notebook was full of hand-drawn diagrams and mind maps that helped me process information visually. None of that would show up in any system logs.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was deep in study mode but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd print out materials and spread them across my floor, making connections between concepts with sticky notes and arrows. Sometimes I'd be working through problems in my head while walking to class or lying in bed. Group study sessions with friends where we'd quiz each other using downloaded materials were super productive but completely invisible to the platform.
Q11: Yes, this happened pretty regularly actually. I'd watch a video once online, then download it and rewatch sections multiple times offline while taking notes. Same with PDF readings, I'd download them, annotate them on my tablet, and never open them in the LMS again. It was more convenient to have everything saved locally so I could access it anytime without logging in. The LMS probably thought I only looked at things once, but that wasn't the case.
Q12: There were definitely some rough patches, especially around midterms when all my courses seemed to have deadlines at the same time. My attendance dropped during those periods because I was trying to catch up on assignments rather than sit through live sessions. I also had some extracurricular commitments that competed for my attention and energy. During those times, I had to make hard choices about where to focus, and sometimes the course took a backseat.
Q13: Workload definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I was overwhelmed, I'd go into survival mode and only engage with what was absolutely necessary for the grade. Fatigue from juggling five online courses meant sometimes I'd log in but not really absorb anything. My extracurricular activities added another layer of time pressure. I tried to manage it all, but there were weeks where I was just treading water rather than really diving deep into the material.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd technically be logged in and watching, but I wasn't really processing the information. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. It's like my body was going through the motions but my brain wasn't fully present. Those sessions didn't really help me learn much.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on some assignments, especially the ones that seemed overwhelming or confusing at first glance. Sometimes I'd avoid starting because I wasn't sure where to begin, and that uncertainty felt paralyzing. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I had more energy or clarity. Looking back, starting earlier would have helped, but in the moment, avoidance felt like self-preservation. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, watching videos, checking rubrics, and submitting work. Without deadlines, I'd probably drift and focus on other things. The urgency of a due date pushed me to prioritize and engage more intensely. It wasn't the healthiest pattern, but it got me through. My activity probably spiked dramatically right before things were due.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my engagement, especially when it was encouraging or showed me where I could improve. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going and try harder on the next assignment. When feedback was critical but constructive, I'd go back to the materials to understand where I went wrong. Sometimes delayed feedback made it hard to stay connected to the course because I'd already moved on mentally by the time I got it.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact on how I participated. When modules were clearly organized and I could see the path from start to finish, I felt more in control. Weekly rhythms helped me plan my time, even if I didn't always stick to the plan perfectly. If things felt scattered or the expectations were unclear, I'd disengage a bit because it was harder to know where to focus my energy.
Q19: Having visual materials like video lectures and diagrams made engagement much easier for me given my learning style. Clear deadlines and organized modules also helped a lot. What made it harder was when there were too many different platforms or tools to navigate, that fragmented my attention. Also, when assignments had vague instructions, I'd spend more time confused than actually learning, which was frustrating and demotivating.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to actually produce the work. Maybe they're watching videos and reading materials but don't feel confident enough to submit assignments. Or they could be dealing with perfectionism, wanting everything to be perfect before turning it in. Life circumstances could also play a role, like having the time to browse but not the focused energy to complete substantial work.
Q21: That actually resonates with my experience somewhat. Because I did a lot of studying offline with downloaded materials and external resources, my LMS activity didn't always reflect my actual effort. Someone could be learning effectively through methods that don't leave digital footprints. Also, efficiency matters, some people can grasp concepts quickly without needing to rewatch videos multiple times. Low activity doesn't necessarily mean low engagement or learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like login frequency, time spent on videos, and submission timestamps. They're good at capturing the digital trail of engagement. But they miss so much, like whether someone was actually focused while watching a video, or all the offline studying that happens. They can't measure understanding, motivation, or the quality of learning. It's like seeing the shadow of engagement rather than the full picture.
Q23: For me, being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions for a grade. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts, and feeling invested in my own learning. True engagement happens when I'm mentally present and actively processing information, whether that's during a video lecture or while sketching out diagrams in my notebook. It's about quality of attention, not just quantity of time.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my resilience and how I've learned to bounce back from setbacks. It wouldn't capture the CBT techniques I've been using to manage my approach to challenges and keep my stress low. My discussions with classmates outside the platform, my handwritten notes, my moments of genuine curiosity, none of that shows up in the data. The emotional journey of learning, the small victories and the growth mindset I've developed, that's invisible but so important to who I am as a student.
